title: Te Pūtaiao o Te Awa: The Living Science of Our River
subject: Science
culturalContext: This unit is deeply connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the ancestral river that flows through our rohe. Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu traditionally monitored and protected this awa using scientific observation methods passed down through generations, particularly focusing on the teachings of local kaumātua Hōri Waititi who documented traditional water quality indicators through ngā tohu o te taiao (environmental signs).
learningObjectives: Measure and analyze water quality parameters (pH, turbidity, temperature) using both traditional Māori indicators and modern scientific instruments
Compare and contrast traditional Ngāti Kahungunu monitoring methods with contemporary scientific approaches for assessing river health
Create a multimedia presentation documenting at least three traditional tohu (signs) used by local iwi to assess Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku's wellbeing
Propose evidence-based conservation actions for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku that integrate mātauranga Māori with modern ecological science

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua to learn traditional karakia and protocols for approaching the river. They observe and record initial environmental conditions using both sensory observation (traditional method) and digital thermometers/PH strips (modern method).

activities.title: Ngā Tohu o Te Taiao
activities.description: Working with local knowledge holders, students learn to identify traditional indicators of river health: presence of kōura (freshwater crayfish), clarity of water (maheni vs pango), riparian vegetation health, and insect life. Students create detailed field sketches and notes.

activities.title: He Aha te Pūtaiao?
activities.description: Students conduct water quality testing using scientific equipment (turbidity tubes, chemical test kits) at three points along the awa. They compare these quantitative measurements with qualitative traditional observations, analyzing correlations between methods.

activities.title: Te Hītori o Te Awa
activities.description: Students examine historical photographs and maps of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku from the local marae archives, tracking changes over time and identifying human impacts through both scientific data and oral histories from kaumātua.

activities.title: Te Rautaki Whakaora
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop conservation proposals for the awa that integrate traditional knowledge (e.g., planting native species identified by elders) with scientific approaches (e.g., monitoring water quality changes).

resources: Kaumātua Hōri Waititi (local knowledge holder specializing in traditional environmental indicators)
'Ngā Tohu o Te Awa' resource kit from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
Water quality testing equipment from NIWA's Rivers and Us program
Historical archives and maps from Māhia Marae documenting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku's changes over 100 years

tasks: Scientific field report comparing traditional and modern water quality assessment methods
Multimedia presentation documenting three traditional tohu and their scientific correlations
Conservation action proposal with integrated mātauranga Māori and scientific justification

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Understanding about science
Living World: Ecology - investigate the impact of natural events and human actions on a New Zealand ecosystem
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - investigate the water cycle and its dynamic relationships with other Earth systems
NZC Key Competencies: Participating and contributing, Relating to others
